text: Recent years have seen a dramatic proliferation of location-based services, leaving untrusted service providers with the unpalatable fact that their infrastructure is used to collect massive amounts of highly precise spatial data on countless individuals. We investigate this privacy problem in depth and introduce a formal privacy concept that we call geo-indistinguishability for location-based systems. The basic idea is that sensitive information about a user’s actual location can be protected while less sensitive approximate location information can be published that is sufficient for providing the user with a desired service. We formalize the concept of protecting the location of a user up to a certain radius r. The resulting privacy definition is related to differential privacy and scales with the parameter r providing a higher level of privacy for larger values of r. We describe a perturbation-based mechanism for the protection of users’ location information by intentionally introducing randomness in the data sent to service providers, and explore its applicability to a Location-Based Service scenario. We compare this approach with other anonymization techniques as presented in the related work section. In particular, we show through empirical evaluation that the location protection that we obtain, while supporting high levels of utility to users, achieves a higher level of privacy than that of the other standalone methods.
